9e58ed040a8e416da41cf2f665107c25c31fdca2.text:
Endocrine Module – Introduction to Hormones
At the end of the lecture students should be able to:
Distinguish between endocrine signaling and other forms of intercellular communication
Classify hormones based on their structure, solubility, & storability
Describe catecholamine biosynthesis
Describe the initial step of steroid biosynthesis
Explain the effects of carrier proteins on hormone activity and half-life
Classify hormone receptors based on their subcellular location and mechanism of action
Explain the factors that influence cellular sensitivity to hormones and cellular response to hormones
los: Distinguish between endocrine signaling and other forms of intercellular communication
Classify hormones based on their structure, solubility, & storability
Describe catecholamine biosynthesis
Describe the initial step of steroid biosynthesis
Explain the effects of carrier proteins on hormone activity and half-life
Classify hormone receptors based on their subcellular location and mechanism of action
Explain the factors that influence cellular sensitivity to hormones and cellular response to hormones

dbe7f7661e2488be4d5d9fd9098d62fddfe19224.text:
Endocrine Module – Hypothalamic-Pituitary Relationships (part 1)
At the end of the lecture students should be able to:
Describe the anatomical connections between the hypothalamus and pituitary gland, and explain the functional interactions between them
Identify the nuclei of the hypothalamus and the hormones they secrete
Describe the anatomical organization and histology of the pituitary gland, and the cells and structures therein
Identify the pituitary hormones, and explain how specific hypothalamic hormones regulate the release of each one
Describe the feedback loops that regulate hormone secretion, and explain the origin and targets for each loop
Predict the changes in hypothalamic, pituitary, and target organ hormone secretion if one particular hormone is increased or decreased (all iterations)
los: Describe the anatomical connections between the hypothalamus and pituitary gland, and explain the functional interactions between them
Identify the nuclei of the hypothalamus and the hormones they secrete
Describe the anatomical organization and histology of the pituitary gland, and the cells and structures therein
Identify the pituitary hormones, and explain how specific hypothalamic hormones regulate the release of each one
Describe the feedback loops that regulate hormone secretion, and explain the origin and targets for each loop
Predict the changes in hypothalamic, pituitary, and target organ hormone secretion if one particular hormone is increased or decreased (all iterations)

9005fabba57b2fc4051c9df35e97e30024676068.text:
Endocrine Module – Hypothalamic-Pituitary Relationships (part 2)
At the end of the lecture students should be able to:
Hypothalamic-pituitary-somatic (growth hormone) axis:
Identify the signaling pathways for growth hormone (GH), from hypothalamus to pituitary to liver to target tissues
Explain the physiological effects of GH and IGF-1, in particular the effects on tissue growth, serum [glucose], and on the liver, adipose tissue, and muscle tissue
Explain how growth hormone secretion is regulated, including dynamic changes on circadian and lifetime timescales
Identify the physiologic stimulators and inhibitors of growth hormone signaling
Describe the effects of altered or disordered growth hormone signaling and the associated conditions
los: Identify the signaling pathways for growth hormone (GH), from hypothalamus to pituitary to liver to target tissues
Explain the physiological effects of GH and IGF-1, in particular the effects on tissue growth, serum [glucose], and on the liver, adipose tissue, and muscle tissue
Explain how growth hormone secretion is regulated, including dynamic changes on circadian and lifetime timescales
Identify the physiologic stimulators and inhibitors of growth hormone signaling
Describe the effects of altered or disordered growth hormone signaling and the associated conditions

66fafd28e74740ed2818597c6a020620d04a5695.text:
Endocrine Module – Adrenal Hormone Synthesis & Action video
At the end of the video students should be able to:
Recognize the structure of steroid hormones and understand the nomenclature used to describe them
Describe the synthesis steps shared by all steroid hormones
Differentiate between layers of the adrenal cortex, the enzymes that they express, and the hormones that they secrete
Describe the biosynthesis pathways for aldosterone, cortisol, and DHEA
Know the mechanisms that facilitate circulation and inactivation of aldosterone, cortisol, and DHEA, and explain their effects on half-life
Understand the primary mechanisms of action of aldosterone and cortisol on their target cells
los: Recognize the structure of steroid hormones and understand the nomenclature used to describe them
Describe the synthesis steps shared by all steroid hormones
Differentiate between layers of the adrenal cortex, the enzymes that they express, and the hormones that they secrete
Describe the biosynthesis pathways for aldosterone, cortisol, and DHEA
Know the mechanisms that facilitate circulation and inactivation of aldosterone, cortisol, and DHEA, and explain their effects on half-life
Understand the primary mechanisms of action of aldosterone and cortisol on their target cells

7bb0e30f51b019c2a0ea780c4cfd5d704cde81f2.text:
Endocrine Module – Sex Steroid Synthesis & Action video
At the end of the video students should be able to:
Differentiate between C21 steroids and androgen and estrogen hormones
List the androgen and estrogen hormones and identify their relative affinity for their receptors
Identify the sources of androgen and estrogen hormones
Describe the biosynthesis pathways for androgens and estrogens
Know the mechanisms that facilitate circulation and inactivation of androgens and estrogens, and explain their effects on half-life
Understand the primary mechanisms of action of sex steroid hormones on their target cells
los: Differentiate between C21 steroids and androgen and estrogen hormones
List the androgen and estrogen hormones and identify their relative affinity for their receptors
Identify the sources of androgen and estrogen hormones
Describe the biosynthesis pathways for androgens and estrogens
Know the mechanisms that facilitate circulation and inactivation of androgens and estrogens, and explain their effects on half-life
Understand the primary mechanisms of action of sex steroid hormones on their target cells

5f2b8ba668836b0e6f44424e6cf0b3d19eb32d0f.text: 2024 POSTERIOR PITUITARY Learning Objectives Thomas G. Sherman, PhD 1. REVIEW the basics of hypothalamic endocrinology; especially Dr. Watson’s lectures on Hypothalamic-Pituitary Relationships. 2. NAME the two major peptide hormones secreted by the posterior pituitary and DESCRIBE their physiological functions. 3. UNDERSTAND the physiological advantage of amidation for peptide hormones. 4. LIST the three receptor subtypes and their anatomical locations for the vasopressin receptor. 5. DESCRIBE how the three vasopressin receptors and the oxytocin receptor link to second messenger pathways. 6. DESCRIBE how circumventricular organs are involved in homeostasis. 7. DEFINE osmotic threshold 8. DESCRIBE the osmoreceptor-vasopressin-renal reflex 9. DISCUSS the relationships between plasma vasopressin concentration and plasma osmolality. 10. DISCUSS the relationships between plasma vasopressin concentration and urine osmolality. 11. DEFINE maximal antidiuresis and the motivational drives that can counter dehydration. 12. DESCRIBE the physiological significance of activated cold-sensitive oropharyngeal receptors. 13. DESCRIBE the impact of blood pressure changes on vasopressin secretion. 14. UNDERSTAND the relationship between blood volume and osmoregulated vasopressin secretion. 15. DISCUSS the relative roles and tissue distribution of vasopressin V and V receptors. 1a 2 16. UNDERSTAND why the osmotic threshold for thirst is 5–10 mOsm/kg greater than the osmotic threshold for AVP secretion. 17. DEFINE central diabetes insipidus and UNDERSTAND its multiple possible etiologies. 18. DESCRIBE how central diabetes insipidus is treated clinically. 19. DESCRIBE how peptidergic drugs are administered. 20. DEFINE nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. 21. DESCRIBE how nephrogenic diabetes insipidus is treated clinically. 22. DESCRIBE gestational diabetes insipids and HOW it is treated clinically. 23. DESCRIBE the anatomy and physiology of the suckling reflex. 24. UNDERSTAND the anatomical basis for milk ejection via various sensory systems. 25. DESCRIBE the dynamic role of uterine oxytocin receptor expression in parturition. 26. DESCRIBE the different second messenger pathways coupled to oxytocin receptor activation in mammary epithelium and uterine myometrium. 27. DESCRIBE why oxytocin receptor antagonists or ineffective at stopping premature labor. 28. DEFINE galactogogue 29. DESCRIBE the roles of oxytocin and vasopressin in pair bonding and partner preference.

6cbedb2503a107eb578949696307de41ef02f79b.text:
Endocrine Module
Georgetown University School of Medicine
Educational Objectives
Lecturer: Rita Santi, MD
Lecture: Disorders of the Pituitary Gland
Robbins (10th ed) text: Chapter 24, The Endocrine System
The student will be able to:
•	Review the normal anatomy and histology of the pituitary.
•	Characterize the following clinical syndromes:
o 	Hyperpituitarism
o 	Hypopituitarism
o 	Local mass effect
•	Classify pituitary neuroendocrine tumors (formerly known as pituitary adenomas) based on functional/immunohistochemical features
For each subtype characterize:
o 	Signs and symptoms and clinical manifestations
o 	Light microscopic features
o 	Electron microscopic features (where pertinent)
•	Discuss the differential diagnosis for additional space-occupying lesions of the pituitary: Lymphocytic hypophysitis, idiopathic granulomatous hypophysitis, pituicytomas, Rathke cyst, empty sella syndrome.

b3761e03ffe5f0fa76d8e327305d9fa48cc9df30.text: ENDOCRINE MODULE - HISTOLOGY Thyroid / Parathyroid/ Adrenal LEARNING OBJECTIVES  Know the Location, Developmental Origin, Structure and Cell Types in the Thyroid Gland, the Parathyroid Glands, and the Adrenal Glands.  Understand the Secretory Mechanisms for Thyroid, Parathyroid and Adrenal Hormones, and their Endocrine Regulation.  Know the Functions of Thyroid, Parathyroid and Adrenal Hormones and the Human Health Disorders Associated with their Over- and Under-production.

3e20a565bd1beef5084b0a49fea5d2f92d56f44c.text:
Thyroid hormone synthesis AND ACTION
Learning Objectives
At the end of the lecture students should be able to:
Recognize the structure of thyroid hormones, and their precursors.
State the human nutritional requirement for iodide.
State which tissues can take up iodine.
State the cell types and their functions in the thyroid.
Describe the mechanism by which iodide is concentrated in the thyroid.
Give examples of inhibitors of the iodide pump and their mechanism of action.
Describe the enzymes involved in iodide oxidation and organification.
Describe the structure and role of thyroglobulin in thyroid hormone synthesis.
Contrast thyroid hormone storage with storage of other hormones.
Detail mechanisms of thyroglobulin degradation, iodide recycling and hormone release.
Discuss thyroid hormone transport and activation.
State the forms of thyroid hormone receptor and their locations within cells.
Discuss how thyroid hormone receptors activate or inhibit transcription.
Give examples of genes regulated by thyroid hormone.
Give examples of diseases involving hyper- and hypo- thyroid states.
At the end of the course, students should be able to integrate the molecular mechanisms of thyroid hormone synthesis and transcriptional control with the physiological role of thyroid hormone in the human.
los: Recognize the structure of thyroid hormones, and their precursors.
State the human nutritional requirement for iodide.
State which tissues can take up iodine.
State the cell types and their functions in the thyroid.
Describe the mechanism by which iodide is concentrated in the thyroid.
Give examples of inhibitors of the iodide pump and their mechanism of action.
Describe the enzymes involved in iodide oxidation and organification.
Describe the structure and role of thyroglobulin in thyroid hormone synthesis.
Contrast thyroid hormone storage with storage of other hormones.
Detail mechanisms of thyroglobulin degradation, iodide recycling and hormone release.
Discuss thyroid hormone transport and activation.
State the forms of thyroid hormone receptor and their locations within cells.
Discuss how thyroid hormone receptors activate or inhibit transcription.
Give examples of genes regulated by thyroid hormone.
Give examples of diseases involving hyper- and hypo- thyroid states.

At the end of the course, students should be able to integrate the molecular mechanisms of thyroid hormone synthesis and transcriptional control with the physiological role of thyroid hormone in the human.

f1ba29dffe656bfd9873c8e0fd9b3fdc3aa2c12a.text:
At the end of the course, students should be able to integrate the molecular mechanisms of thyroid hormone synthesis and transcriptional control with the physiological role of thyroid hormone in the human.
Thyroid Hormones
Structure of Thyroid Hormones.
Made only in thyroid tissue by the sequential addition of iodine to tyrosine to form:
Two iodinated tyrosines are coupled to form:
In the periphery T4 can converted to T3 and reverse T3
Iodine Transport
Iodine is transported into a thyroid follicle cell by a pump on the basolateral membrane. This symport transports both Na+ and I- into cells. A small percentage of iodine can enter and leave by diffusion.
The I- pump requires the energy dependent Na+/K+ pump and is thus inhibited by ouabain.
In humans the ratio of iodine inside a thyroid follicle cell to that in serum (the T:S ratio) is approximately 25, and is a reflection of the activity of the pump.
Competitive inhibitors of the iodide pump that are transported into the follicular cells include thiocyanate (SCN-),  perchlorate (ClO4-), perrhenate (ReO4-) and pertechnetate (TcO4-). The inhibition of iodide transport by these inhibitors is most pronounced when iodide levels are low. Radioactive derivatives of these inhibitors (esp. pertechnetate) can be used for radiographic imaging of the thyroid. Perchlorate has been used to treat hyperthyroidism.
Anti-thyroid drugs such as propylthiouracil (PTU) do not inhibit the pump.
The activity of the pump is controlled by the pituitary hormone thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH).  Animals chronically stimulated with TSH have a T:S ratio of about 500, hypophysectomized animals have a T:S ratio of about 5.
The TSH receptor increases the activity of the iodide pump by increasing cAMP levels in cells (G-protein-coupled, adenyl cyclase). Other effects on thyroid cells may be due to phospholipase A2 activation.
Since the follicular lumen has the same electrochemical characteristics as the exterior of the cell, iodide rapidly travels down the electrochemical gradient to the apical membrane and into the lumen by the action of pendrin an anion exchange protein. Pendrin is the protein that is mutated in Pendred Syndrome that is characterized by deafness and decreased incorporation of iodide into thyroglobulin (organification).
Iodide oxidation
Iodide oxidation can only take place in the thyroid  (other tissues such as the salivary glands, mammary gland, chorion, and stomach can take up and concentrate iodide but cannot oxidize it).
Thyroperoxidase (TPO) is a heme containing glycosylated enzyme that has an absolute requirement for hydrogen peroxide.
H2O2 is produced by an NADPH-dependent enzyme resembling cytochrome C reductase.
Occurs on the luminal surface.
The thiourea drugs such as propylthiouracil (PTU) inhibit oxidation.
TSH increases the synthesis of thyroperoxidase.
Iodination of tyrosine
This is the process of fixing iodine and is termed organification.
Iodination of tyrosine residues on luminal thyroglobulin yields 3-monoiodotyrosine (MIT) which is then iodinated at the 5 position to give 3,5-diiodotyrosine (DIT). This is catalyzed by thyroperoxidase.
Free tyrosine can be iodinated to MIT but cannot be incorporated into the polypeptide chain of a protein since a tRNA does not recognize it.
Coupling of iodotyrosils
Coupling of iodotyrosils in thyroglobulin yields:
MIT + DIT = triiodothyronine (T3)
DIT + DIT = tetraiodothyronine (T4)
bound to thyroglobulin.
The coupling reaction does not cleave the peptide bond - it leaves behind dehydroalanine.
Catalyzed by thyroperoxidase. Inhibited by thiourea drugs such as propylthiouracil (PTU).
Structure of Thyroglobulin
A glycosylated homodimer
On average, the thyroglobulin dimer contains 134 tyrosine residues, 5 MIT, 4.5 DIT, 2.5 T4, 0.7 T3.
Many weeks supply of hormone stored in the extracellular colloid.
Release of T3 and T4
Stimulated by TSH due to activation of adenylate cyclase.
Occurs by the complete hydrolysis of thyroglobulin.
Thyroglobulin is engulfed by phagocytosis, these vesicles then fuse with lysosomes to give secondary lysosomes (phagolysosomes).
Hydrolysis of thyroglobulin in the secondary lysosomes yields the iodinated amino acid derivatives T3, T4, DIT and MIT.
There is some specific deiodination of T4 to T3, however most deiodination of T4 occurs in the periphery.  T3 and T4 are released from the cell.
DIT and MIT are deiodinated by a deiodinase, which requires NADPH, and the products recycled.
30% of dietary iodide is absorbed i.e. about 150 g required for the average daily synthesis of T4 and T3 containing 50 g iodide.
Control of Thyroid hormone synthesis by excess levels of iodide.
Counter intuitively, increased iodide can inhibit T3 and T4 production
Iodide treatment is often used clinically to prevent thyroid storm.
Multiple, poorly understood mechanisms are involved.
Short term effects (Wolfe-Chaikoff Effect)
Transient block in organification due to high intracellular levels of iodide.
Relieved after 48 hours due to increased export of iodide.
Medium term Effects
Increased organification leading to increased MIT and DIT but decreased T3/T4
Decreased release of T3/T4 possibly due to decreased T3/T4 content of thyroglobulin.
Cells recover after 7-10 days.
Long term effects
In unusual cases, the iodide block becomes permanent and a goiter and hypothyroidism develop.
These events are probably due to some underlying defect in the thyroid.
The mechanism of recovery from iodide inhibition may involve iodinated arachidonic acid.
Transport of T3 and T4.
Over 99% of T3 and T4 are bound to the carrier proteins thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) (~70%), thyroxine-binding prealbumin (TBPA) (10-15%), and albumin (~ 15-20%).
TBG has 100x the affinity for T3 and T4 compared with TBPA.
Plasma t1/2 of T3 is 1.5 days, of T4 is 6.5 days.
For clinical purposes it is important to measure free as well as total hormone levels since many agents alter TBG levels and thus total hormone without altering free hormone levels.
The liver synthesizes TBG, estrogen increases TBG while androgens and glucocorticoids decrease TBG. Phenytoin and salicylates compete for binding to TBG.
Conversion of T4 to T3
Occurs mainly in the periphery.
Thyroxine deiodinase converts T4 to T3 and to reverse T3 (rT3).
T3 has a 10x higher affinity for the thyroid hormone receptor than T4 i.e. T3 is the most physiologically important thyroid hormone.
rT3 binds to the receptor very weakly- its function, if any, is not known.  Large amounts are made during chronic disease, carbohydrate starvation and in the foetus
PTU inhibits this conversion.
Feedback regulation
T3 inhibits thyroid simulating hormone (TSH) from the pituitary.
T3 probably inhibits thyrotropin releasing hormone (TRH) synthesis and release from the hypothalamus.
Conversely low T3 levels stimulate TRH release.
TRH stimulates TSH release.
Somatostatin inhibits TSH release. (It also inhibits growth hormone {somatotropin} release).
Growth hormone brings about growth responses by inducing the production of insulin like growth factor (IGF, somatomedin) by the liver.
High levels of IGF stimulate somatostatin release.
Thus treatment of children with growth hormone to correct small stature can result in hypothyroidism.
Mechanism of Action
T3 circulates bound to TBG, TBPA and albumin
Free T3 (and free T4) can enter cells and bind to the thyroid hormone receptor (TR).
T3 is usually the active hormone since it binds 10x better to the TR than T4.
The TR is a nuclear receptor in both the absence and presence of hormone.
TR is bound to specific DNA sequences termed thyroid response elements (TRE) whether hormone is present or not.
In the absence of hormone the TR represses transcription.
In the presence of hormone the receptor activates transcription by recruiting transcriptional coactivators that interact with the initiation complex.
TR can bind to DNA either as a homodimer or as a heterodimer with RXR.
The RXR-TR heterodimer is usually most active.
The Thyroid Hormone Receptor
Two genes encode thyroid hormone receptors, TR and TR.
The two receptors are expressed differentially both in different tissues and during development.
There are two TR splice variants, TR and TR; both are active receptors with differential expression.
There are two major splice variants of TR, TR and TR. These splice variants do not bind hormone and their function is unknown.
Hormone binding domain ~27,000 Daltons.
DNA binding domain ~10,000 Daltons (2 zinc-coordinated cysteine fingers).
Member of the retinoid/thyroid/steroid hormone superfamily of receptors.
Most active form is a heterodimer of RXR and TR.
TR activation of transcription
As stated above TR binds to a TRE mainly as a heterodimer with RXR.
TREs consist of inverted (palindromic) or direct repeats of the consensus sequence A G G T C A.
When present as direct repeats, the repeats are usually separated by 4 base pairs:
TREs are found in the growth hormone gene. This explains the effects of thyroid hormone on growth.
TREs are found in the cardiac sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase gene SERCA2. SERCA2 controls the uptake of Ca2+ into the endoplasmic reticulum, a rate-limiting event in muscle contraction. That is, T3 increases SERCA2 and thus heart rate.
T3 also increases heart rate by increasing the transcription of specific isoforms of myosin. Again, these genes contain TREs.
TR inhibition of transcription
DNA sequences termed nTREs mediate transcriptional repression.
TR usually binds to nTREs as a monomer.
The mechanism of inhibition is less well characterized than that of activation.
nTREs are found in genes such as those for the TSH  and  subunits.
Biological Effects
Thyroid hormone increases oxygen consumption. May be due to the known TH effect of increasing the number of Na+/K+ ATPase molecules since it has been suggested that this pump is the major energy drain in most cells.
TH enhances general protein synthesis giving a positive nitrogen balance.
Amphibian metamorphosis (tadpole to frog) is dependent on TH.
TH required for normal human growth and development. Lack of TH postnatally results in deficient growth and brain development. TH replacement therapy at birth allows normal development.
Selected Diseases
Synopsis
Thyroid HormoneS
pumped into thyroid, requires ATP.  Some diffusion.
Pump controlled by TSH (TSI in Grave's disease).
2 classes of inhibitor--perchlorate and thiocyanate types.
is oxidized by thyroperoxidase, only in thyroid, requires peroxide, NADPH.
Oxidation occurs on luminal surface, inhibited by thiourea drugs such as PTU.
Thyroperoxidase forms MIT and DIT in thyroglobulin.
MIT and DIT fuse to form T3; DIT and DIT to form T4 due to thyroperoxidase.
Hydrolysis of thyroglobulin releases T4, T3, MIT, and DIT.
Hydrolysis stimulated by TSH, inhibited by iodine (the Wolff-Chiakoff effect).
TH bound by TBG and TBPA, 99.97% T3 bound, 99.7% T4 bound.
t1/2 T3 = 1.5 days, t1/2 T4 = 6.5 days.
T4 converted to T3 in periphery by deiodinase, inhibited by PTU.
T3 inhibits TSH release from the pituitary, low T3 stimulates release.
T3 inhibits TRH release and synthesis from the hypothalamus.
Somatostatin inhibits TSH release (also inhibits growth hormone release).
TH increases O2 consumption perhaps through Na+/K+ ATPase.
TH gives positive nitrogen balance.
TH required for normal growth and development.
TH has an intracellular receptor (TR): T3 binds 10x as well as T4.
2 TR genes,  and ; each have splice variants; all are differentially regulated.
TR belongs to the steroid/thyroid/retinoic acid superfamily of nuclear receptors.
TR binds DNA in the presence and absence of hormone.
The TR represses transcription through a TRE in the absence of hormone.
The TR activates transcription through a TRE in the presence of hormone.
The TR represses transcription through a nTRE in the presence of hormone.
Hormone recruits coactivators to the receptor that interact with the initiation complex.
Grave's disease is the most common form of hyperthyroidism.
Grave's disease is due to receptor-activating autoimmune antibodies against the TSH receptor.
Symptoms of hyperthyroidism include proptosis, heat intolerance, tremor, weight loss.
Hashimoto's thyroiditis is due to antibodies against thyroglobulin, it leads to hypothyroidism.
Symptoms of hypothyroidism include cold intolerance, weight gain, cold clammy skin.

8f4ea5562f9d7b6b298e7567f9a96894ad636b60.text:
ENDOCRINE MODULE
GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
Lecture: Disease of the Thyroid and Parathyroid
Lecturer: Jacqueline Wieneke, MD
Robbins (10th ed): Chapter 24, The Endocrine System
The student will be able to:
Describe the signs and symptoms associated with hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism and apply these to a clinical case.
Compose a differential diagnosis for hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism.
Apply appropriate laboratory tests results in the work up of a clinical case.
For the following thyroid diseases, describe the pathophysiology, pathologic findings, complications and clinical signs and symptoms.
Diffuse toxic, simple and multinodular goiter
Hashimoto thyroiditis
Subacute thyroiditis
Reidel thyroiditis
Thyroglossal duct cyst
•	Thyroid neoplasms:
o 	Discuss general features of thyroid neoplasms and how clinical features may aid in the diagnosis.
o 	List the common benign and malignant neoplasms.
o 	For each of the following characterize according to age, presentation,
gross and microscopic features, treatment and outcome:
Follicular adenoma
Follicular carcinoma
Papillary carcinoma
Medullary carcinoma
Anaplastic carcinoma
Discuss clinical features of hypoparathyroidism and primary, secondary and tertiary hyperparathyroidism and apply this knowledge to a clinical case.
Differentiate between parathyroid hyperplasia and adenoma and correlate with clinical findings.
List the clinical and pathologic features of parathyroid carcinoma

50705d73fa8a329c39e9cadb73a274fdaa533081.text:
LECTURE end13: Diagnosis and Treatment of Pituitary and Thyroid Disorders
DISCIPLINE: DRT & Pharmacology
LECTURER: Dr. Jacqueline Jonklaas
ENDOCRINE MODULE — GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
Part 1: Pituitary Disorders
LECTURE 8: Diagnosis and Treatment of Pituitary Disorders
DISCIPLINE: DRT/Pharmacology
LECTURER: Dr. Jacqueline Jonklaas DATE: April 29, 2024
ENDOCRINE MODULE
GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
The student will be able to:
Pituitary LECTURE
Recognize that pituitary disorders can involve overproduction or underproduction of pituitary hormones and recognize the associated hormonal profiles and symptoms.
Recognize that pituitary tumors can be associated with non-hormonal symptoms associated with their growth and compression or destruction of surrounding tissues.
List the spectrum of therapeutic options available for patients with pituitary disorders that can involve overproduction or underproduction of pituitary hormones.
Describe how laboratory testing can be used in order to diagnose pituitary disorders associated with over-production of prolactin (prolactinoma).
List the therapeutic options for a patient who has been diagnosed with a prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma).
Describe how laboratory testing can be used in order to diagnose pituitary disorders associated with over-production of growth hormone (acromegaly).
List the therapeutic options for a patient who has been diagnosed with a growth hormone-secreting pituitary adenoma (acromegaly).
Describe how laboratory testing can be used in order to diagnose pituitary disorders associated with overproduction of ACTH (Cushing’s disease).
List the therapeutic options for a patient who has been diagnosed with an ACTH-producing pituitary adenoma (Cushing’s disease).
Recognize the clinical presentation of i) TSH-secreting and ii) non-functional pituitary adenomas
Describe the treatment options for i) TSH-secreting and ii) non-functional pituitary adenomas
List of need to know drugs
Part 2: Thyroid Disorders
LECTURE 14: Diagnosis and Treatment of Thyroid Disorders
DISCIPLINE: DRT & Pharmacology
LECTURER: Dr. Jacqueline Jonklaas DATE: May 13, 2024
ENDOCRINE MODULE
GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
The student will be able to:
Identify the clinical features of hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism.
Identify the important components of the physical examination and expected findings in a patient with hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism.
Describe and prioritize the laboratory tests and imaging studies used in evaluation of the patient with suspected thyroid disease.
Interpret results of different laboratory tests and imaging studies and associate them with the most likely thyroid disease state.
Identify the specific medical conditions that occur when pathologic changes affect the thyroid gland and result in hormone deficiencies or excesses.
Analyze how endocrine drugs may be used to normalize the endocrine feedback loop involving the thyroid gland.
Describe how endocrine drugs can be used to treat medical conditions that result from under-production or over-production of hormones from the thyroid gland, be aware of their side effects, and explain how these drugs can normalize patient symptoms and functioning.
List of need to know drugs*
Thyroxine, levothyroxine (T4) (synthroid®, levoxyl®, tirosint®)
Liothyronine (cytomel®) (T3)
Methimazole
Propylthiouracil (PTU)
*Brand names (®) are given for information only. You will not be expected to memorize brand names (e.g. cytomel®)

7e2437e1eb7bfaef219877f168bd6d129b85a77a.text:
Endocrine Module (END)
Georgetown University School of Medicine
Educational Objectives
Lecturer: Mary A. Furlong, MD
Lecture: Diabetes Mellitus
Robbins (10th ed): Chapter 24, The Endocrine System
The student will be able to:
Pathogenesis
•	List and define the hyperglycemia syndromes: impaired glucose tolerance and diabetes.
•	Describe the classification of diabetes (Chart from Robbins, page
2 of handout).
•	Discuss factors that may play a role in the pathogenesis of type I diabetes.
•	List mechanisms of beta cell destruction.
•	Characterize the pathologic/metabolic abnormalities of type II diabetes and list factors likely to play a role.
•	Define insulin resistance and discuss the association of obesity.
•	Discuss the hypotheses related to free fatty acids and adipokines in insulin resistance.
Complications of Diabetes
Describe the major metabolic derangements associated with diabetes and its target cell.
Describe the microvascular and macrovascular complications associated with diabetes.
List and discuss the 3 main pathways thought to play a role in the pathogenesis of the complications of diabetes.
Pathologic findings
Describe the pathologic findings in the following organs and correlate with clinical signs and symptoms
Clinical
Describe the pathophysiology of diabetic ketoacidosis, the clinical and physical diagnosis findings.
Characterize the clinical features of type I and type II diabetes.
Describe a patient with long-standing diabetes:
What diseases/pathologic processes is this patient at increased risk of developing?
What laboratory values are you interested in following in this patient?
What parts of the physical exam would you focus on in clinic?

0de4edc2b52e19c1e91fbe8bf522d87aebb59c9d.text:
LECTURES 21 &25: Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes	DISCIPLINE: DRT/Pharmacology
LECTURER: Dr. Natasa Janicic
ENDOCRINE MODULE
GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
The student will be able to:
Define the different types of diabetes and their natural history/pathophysiology.
Explain the regulation of insulin secretion and mechanisms of insulin action.
Describe the different therapeutic targets for insulin and the oral antidiabetic agents.
Explain the mechanism of action and side effects of the oral antidiabetic agents.
Need to Know Drugs: (TRADE NAMES IN PARENTHESES do not need to be remembered for exam, just to enrich your background)
Insulins – know by category (rapid acting, short acting, etc.) and the following specific agents:
Rapid-acting:  	Lispro (Humalog)
Aspart (Novolog)
Short-acting:	Regular
Long-acting:	Glargine (Lantus)
Injectable agents – know by category (incretin-mimetics, etc.) and the following specific agents:
GLP-1 agonists:Exenatide (Byetta), Liraglutide (Victoza), Semaglutide (Rybelsus; Ozempic)
Amylin analog: 	Pramlintide (Symlin)
Oral agents – know by class and mechanism of action (sulfonylureas, biguanides, etc.) and the following specific agents:
Biguanide:	Metformin (Glucophage)
Sulfonylureas:	Glipizide (Glucotrol)
Glyburide (Micronase)
Alpha-glucosidase inhibitor:  Acarbose (Precose)
Thiazolidinedione:	Pioglitazone (Actos)
DPP-4 inhibitor: 	Sitagliptin (Januvia)
SGLT2 inhibitor: 	Canagliflozin (Invokana)

3b8f2b0f139a8c896076b99203fb7edad61a095e.text:
LECTURE: Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis	DISCIPLINE:  PHYS/BIOCH
ENDOCRINOLOGY MODULE
GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
At the end of the session, students will be able to:
DESCRIBE the hormones of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and their feedback relationships.  INDICATE the actions of each of these hormones.
DESCRIBE the stimulators and inhibitors of cortisol secretion.
EXPLAIN the differential metabolic effects of cortisol on fat, protein, and glycogen storage sites when cortisol levels are low versus when they are high.
EXPLAIN the basic pathophysiology of Cushing’s Disease, Cushing’s Syndrome, Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia, and Addison’s Disease.  NAME two symptoms for each condition.

f2e1f3497a80a0a1f64e91b8ab08ba88d28698d3.text:
Endocrine Module (END)
Georgetown University School of Medicine
Educational Objectives
Lecturer: Jacqueline Wieneke, MD
Lecture: Adrenal Disorders
Robbins (10th ed): Chapter 24 The Endocrine System
The student will be able to:
•	Review normal anatomy and histology.
•	Characterize congenital adrenal hyperplasia in terms of: genetics and enzymatic deficiencies, clinical features and pathologic findings
•	Define chronic adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s disease).
o 	List primary and secondary causes.
o 	Clinical features and potential complications
•	Define and describe features of Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome.
•	Describe a patient with Cushing syndrome and list causes.
•	Characterize the etiology and clinical features of primary hyperaldosteronism.
•	Neoplasms:
o 	List clinical features and pathologic findings of adrenal cortical adenoma and carcinoma.
o 	Describe a patient presenting with pheochromocytoma and discuss pathologic findings as well as potential complications.

4efec6f8ebbfdcdffb0260f5c0fe14a5f2f14c42.text:
LECTURE: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adrenal Disorders
COURSE:  DRT/Pharmacology
LECTURER: Dr. Susmeeta Sharma
ENDOCRINE MODULE
GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
The student will understand the following concepts/ideas:
Recognize that the concentrations of hormones from the pituitary and adrenal gland can be measured by laboratory testing in order to define the normal functioning of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and analyze its feedback loops.
Describe the changes in hormone concentrations that occur when there are abnormalities in the functioning of the adrenal gland.
Identify the specific medical conditions that occur when pathologic changes affect the adrenal and result in hormone deficiencies.
Identify the specific medical conditions that occur when pathologic changes affect the adrenal and result in hormone excesses.
Analyze how endocrine drugs may be used to normalize the endocrine feedback loop involving the adrenal gland.
Describe how endocrine drugs can be used to treat medical conditions that result from under-production of hormones from the adrenal gland and explain how these drugs can normalize patient symptoms.
Describe how endocrine drugs can be used to treat medical conditions that result from over-production of hormones from the adrenal gland and explain how these drugs can normalize patient symptoms.
List of need to know drugs

5ed9f33b72e3400bd31bcde25689afd38a1d43f3.text:
LECTURE:Stress, Stress Hormones & Pathophysiology Integration	DISCIPLINE: PHYS
ENDOCRINOLOGY MODULE
GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
At the end of the session, students will be able to:
EXPLAIN the difference between acute stress and chronic stress.
EXPLAIN the central role that hypothalamic CRH plays in mediating stress responses and orchestrating the activities of other neuroendocrine systems.
DESCRIBE the effects of pro-inflammatory cytokines on CRH and stress responses.
EXPLAIN how the stress-induced stress hormone and sympathetic nervous system cascade can result in dysfunction and/or diseases of the immune system, reproductive system, GI tract, the cardiovascular system, and cancer metastasis.
EXPLAIN the possible physiological impacts that stress reduction can have on stress-induced disorders/diseases.

ec623587cf62550a8774bdf1791b4120aa6b38a2.text:
Endocrine Module (END)
Georgetown University School of Medicine
Educational Objectives
Lecture: Pathology END Lab
Lecturer: Mary A. Furlong, MD
The student should:
Identify organs of the endocrine system and discuss the feedback loops that control hormone production.
List causes of hyper and hypo-production of hormones for each organ and correlate with clinical signs and symptoms.
Describe the non-neoplastic disorders of the thyroid, parathyroid, and adrenal and correlate with pathologic findings, clinical signs and symptoms and outcome.
Describe specific neoplasms of each endocrine organ and correlate with clinical signs and symptoms, pathologic features, molecular diagnosis and outcome.

1dd3f22c865d299abe90779dded51d9005cc8294.text: http://www.smbc-comics.com/?id=3152 Module overview • Who am I? ATP AC D1R G s + + cAMP • 2 ‘flipped’ class topics PKA (this week!): PP2A - - watch the 2 videos S293 P P Cx36 ahead of time → come + ? + PP1 to 1 flipped class P T75 - Inter. DARPP-32 session (instead of 2) + + Protein T34 P - + CaM CaMKII AMPA CaN Ca2+ ? NMDA ? • Foundational phase textbooks • General study tips for endocrine Learning objectives • Distinguish between endocrine signaling and other forms of intercellular comm. • Classify hormones based on their structure, solubility, & storability • Describe catecholamine biosynthesis • Describe the initial step of steroid biosynthesis • Explain the effects of carrier proteins on hormone activity and half-life • Classify hormone receptors based on their subcellular location and mechanism of action • Explain the factors that influence cellular sensitivity to hormones and cellular response to hormones The ‘first’ endocrinology experiment Autopsies revealed that the implanted testes only connected to the vasculature of the rooster (but not the nervous system). Therefore, some molecule must be transported in the blood. A.A. Berthold, 1849 Methods of intercellular communication The first therapy using hormones Victor Horsley discovered that removing the thyroid gland caused mixedema in monkeys. In the 1880s-1890s, Horsley & his student George Murray found that either implanting sheep thyroid glands or injecting thyroid extracts reversed the effects of mixedema (and cretinism) in humans. https://youtu.be/rIQ3MpKbW8E?t=1012 Textbook of Medical Physiology, Guyton & Hall Why have a nervous and an endocrine system? What are the nervous and endocrine sys. doing on the left? On the right? Why not use nervous for both? The movies in our eyes (2007), Sci Am Oh hai Signal reception & hormone specificity Target cells: cells that express specific receptors that bind hormone and respond accordingly Hormone receptors: proteins that bind a specific hormone molecule and transduce the signal Receptors create the specificity of hormones – if a cell type does not express the appropriate receptor, the hormone will not initiate signaling that directly influences that cell type. • By virtue of traveling through the bloodstream hormones can access nearly all the cells in the body, but they will only affect the cells that are ‘listening’ for them. Classes of hormones Small molecules structurally related to individual amino acids Classified by Generally chemical structure hydrophilic Chains of amino Many molecules are used as acids intercellular signals via multiple delivery mechanisms (e.g. as neurotransmitters, paracrine signals between nearby cells within an organ, and as hormones throughout the body). Carbon rings Hydrophobic and side chains, built from fatty acids or choleserol Amino acid derivatives * TThhyyrrooxxiinnee ((TT )) 44 Tyrosine Epinephrine Secreted by Small molecules suprarenal structurally related Norepinephrine medulla to individual amino acids Melatonin Dopamine Secreted by hypothalamus Secreted by pineal gland Tryptophan Catecholamine synthesis Catecholamine hormones share a biosynthetic pathway that starts from tyrosine: • Tyrosine hydroxylase is the key enzyme that begins the synthetic pathway (and is the rate-limiting step) • Dopamine may be better known for its neurotransmitter function, but it is also a hormone – often referred to as prolactin inhibiting hormone (PIH) or sometimes by the older name prolactin inhibiting factor • The adrenal gland releases both norepinephrine (aka noradrenaline) and epinephrine (aka adrenaline) • Why do post-ganglionic sympathetic neurons release norepinephrine but not epinephrine? Peptide hormones Pituitary gland: Kidneys: Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) Erythropoietin (EPO) Luteinizing hormone (LH) Reproductive organs: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) Inhibin Chains of amino acids Hypothalamus: Pancreas: ADH, oxytocin, regulatory hormones Insulin, glucagon Pituitary gland: Parathyroid gland: ACTH, growth hormone (GH), Parathyroid hormone (PTH) MSH, prolactin (PRL) C cells of thyroid: Heart: Calcitonin (CT) Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) Adipose tissue: Brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) Leptin Digestive tract: Lymphoid system: Hormones discussed in Chapter 24 Hormones discussed in Chapter 22 Glucagon Insulin https://3d.nih.gov Glycoprotein hormones Several of the glycoprotein hormones are dimers that share the same alpha subunit – TSH, FSH, LH, and hCG. For this family of hormones: • the beta subunit confers specificity for binding to the appropriate receptor • Different glycosylation patterns enhance specificity • the alpha subunit is required for inducing the conformational change that activates the receptor Hydrophilic hormone circulation & action • Most amino acid derivative and peptide hormones are hydrophilic and thus can circulate freely as solutes in the blood • May or may not bind to carrier proteins while they circulate • These types of hormone typically have a short half-life, on the order of minutes to tens of minutes • May be modified or inactivated by enzymes in the plasma, interstitial fluid, or in the liver • excreted by the kidney (if able to enter the renal filtrate) • Due to their inability to cross the plasma membrane, hydrophilic hormones: • act as indirect signaling molecules, with receptors located in the plasma membrane that transduce the signal across the membrane by activating enzymes, opening ion channels, and/or engaging second messenger systems inside the cell • are easily stored in cells in secretory vesicles or granules Indirect signaling to receptors Indirect signaling utilizes a huge variety of transduction mechanisms, including but not limited to: • Ion channel-linked signaling • G protein-linked signaling • Enzyme-linked signaling The transduction step allows for significant amplification of the signal inside the cell – 1 hormone bound to its receptor can trigger formation of thousands of second messengers or enzymatic reactions Effects may be post-translational (& rapid) or transcriptional (& slower) Lipid derivatives Arachidonic acid EXAMPLE: Include: Prostaglandin E Leukotrienes Prostaglandins Thromboxanes Prostacyclins Carbon rings and side chains, built from fatty acids or choleserol Gonads: Suprarenal cortex: EXAMPLE: Androgens Mineralocorticoids Estradiol, an Estrogens Glucocorticoids estrogen Progestins Androgens Cholesterol Kidneys: Calcitriol Steroid synthesis All steroid hormones are synthesized from a cholesterol precursor, beginning with the synthesis of pregnenolone by the enzyme desmolase. • The first groups of steroids are classified as C 21 steroids because they contain 21 carbons – these are the progestins and corticoids • Androgens make up the C hormones 19 • Estrogens make up the C hormones 18 Hydrophobic hormone circulation & action • Hydrophobic hormones do not dissolve easily in the blood; to facilitate circulation, a large fraction of each hormone is bound by carrier proteins • allows for a substantial reserve of bound hormones in the blood • These types of hormone have a longer half-life than hydrophilic hormones, on the order of tens of minutes to hours • due to being protected from degradation/excretion by the carriers • Bound hormones are not available to activate receptors • A fraction of the hormone is dissociated from the carriers at any given time, as described by it’s dissociation constant (K ); this free fraction of d hormone is the active portion that can enter cells and activate receptors • Due to their ability to cross the plasma membrane, hydrophobic hormones: • act as direct signaling molecules, with receptors (frequently transcription factors) located in the cytoplasm or in organelles • are not capable of being stored or accumulated in most cells Direct signaling to receptors Although the ‘canonical’ examples of direct signaling involve activation of intracellular receptors that modulate transcription, it is important to note that hydrophobic hormone signaling is not limited to this mechanism • There are many membrane-bound receptors for hydrophobic hormones, as well as rapid post-translational effects – it’s just receptor-dependent Hormone response curves A cell’s sensitivity to a hormone (as measured by the EC ) can be 50 adjusted in multiple ways: • Different receptors display different affinities for the same hormone • A single receptor may have its affinity modulated by post- translational modification • The number of hormone receptors in the cell’s membrane may be altered Goodman (2001), Adv Physiol Educ Hormone response curves: insulin resistance as an example Petersen & Shulman (2018), Physiol Rev Strategies for studying endocrine function 1) Draw the circuits that control secretion • e.g. hypothalamus → pituitary → target organ → feedback • make predictions for how hormone concentrations will change when one element of the circuit is disrupted 2) Understand the actions of the terminal effector hormones on target tissues and organs 3) Explain how dysregulation of each hormone causes dysfunction in the target tissues and organs • in almost every case, too much or too little hormone is associated with specific disorders and/or diseases • understand the relevant interactions between organ systems necessary to explain the disparate effects of some endocrine disorders 4) Describe the additional sources of regulation (besides the primary circuit) that integrate endocrine function across organ systems

f7da919a6ac0c21c27a71242111201c19f9b0515.text: https://xkcd.com/323/ Learning objectives • Describe the anatomical connections between the hypothalamus and pituitary gland, and explain the functional interactions between them • Identify the nuclei of the hypothalamus and the hormones they secrete • Describe the anatomical organization and histology of the pituitary gland, and the cells and structures therein • Identify the pituitary hormones, and explain how specific hypothalamic hormones regulate the release of each one • Describe the feedback loops that regulate hormone secretion, and explain the origin and targets for each loop • Predict the changes in hypothalamic, pituitary, and target organ hormone secretion if one particular hormone is increased or decreased (all iterations) Endocrine glands The hypothalamus (neural) & pituitary gland (glandular and neural) work together to control most of the other primary endocrine glands • Most hypothalamic and pituitary hormones also act as effector hormones at specific target cells, in addition to their regulatory roles at endocrine glands Hypothalamus & pituitary location The hypothalamus is part of the diencephalon of the brain and sits superior to the pituitary gland (which resides in the sella turcica of the sphenoid bone). They are connected by the infundibulum (aka the pituitary stalk). Hypothalamic nuclei The hypothalamus contains a number of nuclei (clusters of neuron cell bodies). The neurons in each nucleus receive inputs from distinct parts of the brain and release specific hormones. Pituitary anatomy Pituitary histology Where is the pars intermedia? https://digitalhistology.org Step 1 when looking at a diagram (sagittal) of the hypothalamus – establish which side is the anterior side and which side is the posterior side • Anterior is the side closer to the optic chiasm The hypothalamus coordinates the release of hormones in multiple ways The hypophyseal portal system Releasing hormones (-RH) & inhibiting hormones (-IH) are delivered directly from the hypothalamus to the adenohypophysis by the hypophyseal portal system. They are secreted into the first capillary network in the median eminence. The hypothalamic hormones RH = releasing hormone IH = inhibiting hormone Secreted into the portal system: • Gonadotropin-RH (GnRH) • Gonadotropin-IH (GnIH) • Corticotropin-RH (CRH) • Thyrotropin-RH (TRH) • Growth hormone-RH (GHRH) • Somatostatin (aka growth hormone-IH, GHIH) • Prolactin-IH (PIH, aka dopamine) Most releasing and inhibiting hormones are peptides = GHIH Gonadotropin-inhibiting Single chain of 8 Inhibits secretion of FSH and LH by hormone (GnIH) amino acids gonadotropes The hypothalamic nuclei (don’t memorize this) • Medial preoptic nucleus – GnRH • Dorsomedial nucleus – GnIH • Paraventricular nucleus – CRH • PVN & anterior nucleus – TRH • Ventromedial nucleus – GHRH • Periventricular nucleus* Arcuate nucleus – somatostatin (GHIH) • Arcuate nucleus – PIH Direct secretion of hormones into the posterior pituitary via axonal projections The hypothalamic nuclei Secreted into neurohypophysis: • Vasopressin (aka antidiuretic hormone, ADH) • Oxytocin • Both from supraoptic nucleus and paraventricular nucleus Together, these are called the nonapeptides. Histology of the neurohypophysis The axon terminals of hypothalamic neurons swell into eosinophilic structures called Herring bodies, which store and release oxytocin and vasopressin into the extracellular space. From there they diffuse into nearby fenestrated capillaries. Histology of the neurohypophysis At higher magnification the secretory vesicles (granules) are visible in the Herring bodies. The basophilic cell nuclei seen here are pituitary glial cells called pituicytes. EM of secretory cell next to a capillary Fenestrations The circumventricular organs The circumventricular organs are a collection of regions (so named due to proximity to the ventricles of the brain) where the blood-brain barrier is minimal or does not exist. Several of them are important for endocrine function, namely the: • Median eminence • Neurohypophysis • The organum vasculosum of the lamina terminalis (OVLT), which senses blood osmolarity and hormones to regulate vasopressin release • The pineal gland, which releases the hormone melatonin Bentivoglio et al (2018), Front Immunol Functions of antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) Body temperature decrease Body temperature increase Functions of oxytocin* Direct control of endocrine organs via the sympathetic nervous system Direct secretion of hormones into the posterior pituitary via axonal projections Histology of the adenohypophysis The adenohypophysis contains secretory cells classed as basophils (hematoxylin- stained granules) and acidophils (eosin-stained granules), as well as non-secretory supporting cells classed as chromophobes, along with fenestrated capillaries. https://digitalhistology.org Acidophils of the adenohypophysis Acidophil cells release 2 of the 6 anterior pituitary hormones, either growth hormone (GH, from somatotrope cells) or prolactin (PRL, from lactotrope cells). However, H&E staining does not show which cells release which hormone. https://digitalhistology.org Basophils of the adenohypophysis Basophil cells release the other 4 of the 6 anterior pituitary hormones: follicle- stimulating hormone (FSH) or luteinizing hormone (LH, both from gonadotrope cells), adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH, from corticotrope cells), or thyroid- stimulating hormone (TSH, from thyrotrope cells). https://digitalhistology.org Anterior pituitary hormones are peptides or glycoproteins The pituitary’s pars intermedia secretes MSH pars intermedia Hormones of the pars intermedia: • Melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) is produced here The pars intermedia is mostly nonfunctional in adult humans, but functions in fetuses & young children Histology of the pars intermedia The densely packed basophilic cells on the left are from the pars intermedia. Also visible are cysts filled eosinophilic colloid, a characteristic of the pars intermedia. https://digitalhistology.org Summary Negative feedback controls blood [hormone] Example with both a RH and an IH Feedback loops that regulate [hormone] Ultrashort loop negative feedback is inhibition of hypothalamic hormone release driven by the hypothalamic hormones themselves • Axon collaterals • Sets pulsatile rhythm Short loop negative feedback is inhibition of hypothalamic hormone release driven by the hormones released from the anterior pituitary Long loop negative feedback is inhibition of hypothalamic or pituitary hormone release driven by the hormones released from target organs Pulsatile secretion Some hormones are regulated in a more complex fashion – e.g. a gradual increase in [estradiol] as the ovarian cycle progresses causes a switch from negative feedback on GnRH release to positive feedback on GnRH release, creating the surge in luteinizing hormone that triggers ovulation. Autoinhibition (via ultrashort loop feedback) creates a pulsatile secretion pattern. This is important for preventing desensitization to hormones by the target cells. Another example of positive feedback In the context of labor, oxytocin triggers uterine contractions, and the resulting pressure on the cervix triggers more oxytocin release. Hormones have diverse actions Oxytocin (as a neuropeptide and a hormone) also influences pair bonding, social awareness, memory, metabolism, and so on… Today’s public service announcement How exactly is this supposed to work?

4b29a8b0f46f93f8501e6333e45cb9cda7b217c7.text: https://www.smbc-comics.com/comic/the-last-potion Growth hormone learning objectives • Identify the signaling pathways for growth hormone (GH), from hypothalamus to pituitary to liver to target tissues • Explain the physiological effects of GH and IGF-1, in particular the effects on tissue growth, serum [glucose], and on the liver, adipose tissue, and muscle tissue • Explain how growth hormone secretion is regulated, including dynamic changes on circadian and lifetime timescales • Identify the physiologic stimulators and inhibitors of growth hormone signaling • Describe the effects of altered or disordered growth hormone signaling and the associated conditions Growth hormone pathways Growth hormone (GH) is a 191-AA protein released from somatotrophs in the anterior pituitary in response to GHRH (growth hormone releasing hormone). GH acts widely on almost all cells in the body, both directly and indirectly. The indirect actions of growth hormone are caused via GH binding to receptors on hepatocytes that triggers release of somatomedin hormones. There are several somatomedins, the best studied of which is insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1, aka somatomedin C), a 70-AA peptide. The IGF1 receptor is distinct from the GH receptor, and stimulates mitogenic and anabolic processes in many cell types in the body, as well as promotes cell survival. GHRH, GH, & IGF-1 signal transduction GH is small enough to enter the renal filtrate; 50% of GH is bound to growth hormone-binding protein (GHBP) to increase its half-life (still only ~20 minutes) Nearly all IGF-1 circulates bound to insulin-like growth factor- binding protein (IGFBP), giving it a longer half life (~12 hours)* • *IGFBP proteases free IGF from the carrier, activating it GHRH and somatostatin converge on the same signaling pathway GH has high homology to prolactin, and can also bind to the PRL receptor GH and IGF-1 targets GH promotes protein anabolism in the fed state, and glucose-sparing when fasting. The dual roles of growth hormone GH release is stimulated by high serum [AA], but inhibited by high serum [glucose] (*time course) Insulin drives GH receptor expression in the liver, and is thus permissive for GH-driven secretion of IGF-1. Thus, a balanced meal will promote growth. A carb-heavy but protein-light meal will drive energy storage but not growth. Fasting stimulates GH secretion (due to low serum [glucose] & [fatty acid]) at a time when [insulin] is low, driving the hyperglycemic and lipolytic effects of GH. The diabetogenic effect of growth hormone Growth hormone’s context-dependent actions on skeletal muscle and adipose tissue stem in part from its ability to reduce the sensitivity of these tissues to insulin. Put differently, growth hormone induces insulin resistance in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue. Thus, GH has a diabetogenic effect. Between meals this promotes effective use of metabolic substrates. However, disorders that involve excess and prolonged growth hormone secretion are associated with increased incidence of diabetes mellitus. Regulation of the HPS axis The hypothalamic-pituitary-somatic (HPS) axis receives long loop, short loop, and ultrashort loop feedback. Peripheral regulators (e.g. amino acids, glucose, etc.) typically target GHRH neurons, somatostatin (SS, or GHIH) neurons, or both. The dual regulatory pathways of GHRH and SS sharpen the pulsatile nature of GH release, creating a peaks and troughs that are sharp and temporally distinct. Regulation of growth hormone The normal pattern of GH secretion is pulses approx. every 4 hours. Deep sleep and melatonin stimulate GH release, resulting in a particularly large pulse occurring early around midnight (for most people). Exercise also has a potent stimulatory effect on GH secretion. Over the course of a human lifetime, the baseline level of serum [GH] fluctuates. It is low at birth high in childhood, highest in puberty, and then declines to a plateau in adulthood. It declines again in old age (due to sex hormone declines). Regulators of growth hormone signaling Stimulation Inhibition • Decreased serum [glucose] • Increased serum [glucose] • Decreased serum [free fatty acid] • Increased serum [free fatty acid] • Increased serum [amino acids] • Hypercortisolemia • Fasting • chronic or intense stress • Prolonged caloric deprivation • Cushing’s disorders • Deep sleep (slow-wave, stage 4) • Pregnancy • human placental lactogen (hPL) • Exercise (aka human chorionic • Stress somatomammotropin, or hCS) • physiological [cortisol] • Sex steroids (estradiol, testosterone) • Ghrelin Disorders of growth Growth hormone deficiency (aka pituitary dwarfism) stems from deficiency in growth hormone production, leading to proportionate smaller bones. Growth hormone insensitivity (aka Laron syndrome) creates a phenotype similar to pituitary dwarfism, but is caused by mutations in the growth hormone receptor. How would you treat growth hormone deficiency? Lavinia Warren, age 18 (2’ 8”) How would you treat growth hormone insensitivity? Disorders of growth Excess growth hormone prior to puberty is the cause of gigantism. Both dwarfism and gigantism come with associated health risks, but they are more severe for gigantism. Excess growth hormone after puberty causes acromegaly. What could cause these disorders? How would you treat them? Robert Wadlow, age 22 (8’ 11”) Abnormal secretion of growth hormone Pituitary adenomas (macro [>10 mm] or micro [<10 mm]) are benign tumors that may oversecrete specific hormones and/or impair hormone secretion. The second most common type of functional (secreting) adenoma makes GH. Acromegaly examples After epiphyseal closure, the long bones do not grow but other bones may become thicker (typically in the jaw, hands, and feet). Organs and soft tissues also grow, especially connective tissues, causing other impairments. Gupta et al (2024), Front Endocrinol (Lausanne)

5aa7703d95cc821f2d4be7355d6814ac74e77cf2.text: https://xkcd.com/557/ Learning objectives • Recognize the structure of steroid hormones and understand the nomenclature used to describe them • Describe the synthesis steps shared by all steroid hormones • Differentiate between layers of the adrenal cortex, the enzymes that they express, and the hormones that they secrete • Describe the biosynthesis pathways for aldosterone, cortisol, and DHEA • Know the mechanisms that facilitate circulation and inactivation of aldosterone, cortisol, and DHEA, and explain their effects on half-life • Understand the primary mechanisms of action of aldosterone and cortisol on their target cells The adrenal glands Chromaffin cells (postglangionic neuroendocrine cells) in the adrenal medulla releases catecholamine hormones – mainly epinephrine (75-80%), and are the only source of serum [epinephrine]. The adrenal cortex synthesizes and releases a variety of steroid hormones, most notably: • mineralocorticoids (e.g. aldosterone) • glucocorticoids (e.g. cortisol) • androgens (e.g. dehydroepiandrosterone, or DHEA) and androstenedione Organization of the adrenal glands ZG ZF The adrenal cortex is comprised of 3 layers: ZR • the zona glomerulosa, which secretes mineralocorticoids • the zona fasciculata, which secretes glucocorticoids Medulla • the zona reticularis, which secretes androgens https://digitalhistology.org Steroid nomenclature Steroid hormones all share the same basic 4-ringed, planar structure derived from their cholesterol precursor. To track the enzymatic actions during steroid synthesis and appreciate the structural differences between steroid hormones, it is somewhat useful to know their nomenclature: • A, B, C, and D rings • Carbon numbering • Double bond location Steroidogenesis Pregnenolone is the precursor to all steroid hormones; it is synthesized by a cytochrome P450 enzyme in the inner mitochondrial membrane called cholesterol desmolase (also know as P450scc, for ‘side-chain cleavage’). Steroidogenesis Steroid hormones synthesis involves a number of steps: • Liberation of cholesterol from lipid droplets by hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL on figure) • Transfer of cholesterol to the inner mitochondrial membrane by steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) so that it can be converted to pregnenolone by desmolase. This transfer is the rate- limiting step. All of these actions are coordinated • Increased LDLR and P450scc by increased generation of the expression (with some delay) second messenger cAMP in the cytoplasm of steroidogenic cells. Zona glomerulosa steroid synthesis Aldosterone is the most potent mineralocorticoid hormone (by far) • Glucocorticoid hormones are also able to bind and activate mineralocorticoid receptors • deoxycorticosterone is the corticosteroid with the 2nd most potent mineralocorticoid activity A significant fraction of aldosterone circulates freely (~35%), while the rest binds with low affinity to either corticosteroid binding globulin (CBG) or albumin • Half-life is ~20 minutes • Inactivated by glucuronide conjugation in the liver and excreted by the kidneys Zona fasciculata steroid synthesis Cortisol is the primary glucocorticoid hormone in humans (of the “cort-” hormones, cortisol has the highest affinity for the glucocorticoid receptor) ~90% of cortisol circulates bound to the carrier corticosteroid-binding protein (CBG, aka transcortin), with an additional ~6% bound to albumin and the rest free • Half-life is ~70 minutes • Inactivated by glucuronide or sulfate conjugation in the liver and excreted by the kidneys Zona reticularis steroid synthesis The major output of the zona reticularis is DHEA (with androstenedione as a minor product) • DHEA circulates in higher concentration than any other hormone and serves as a precursor for more potent sex steroids DHEA is sulfated to form DHEA-S (inactive) in the reticularis • DHEA circulates bound to albumin (low affinity) • Half-life is 15-30 minutes, excreted by kidneys • DHEA-S also binds to albumin (high affinity) • Half-life is 7-10 hours The reticularis develops and begins secreting DHEA between ages 6-8 years (adrenarche), and peaks mid-20s Adrenal steroid synthesis (combined) Consider how enzymatic deficiencies would alter adrenal function The reticularis does not express 21β-hydroxylase, has enhanced 17,20-lyase activity, and has low 3β-HSD activity Reticularis The fasciculata has low 17,20-lyase activity and the CYP11B1 version of 11β-hydroxylase Fasciculata The glomerulosa does not express 17α-hydroxylase, and is the only site of aldosterone synthase (CYP11B2) expression Glomerulosa Relative concentrations and activities of adrenal steroids Summary of synthetic pathways Mechanisms of aldosterone action Mineralocorticoid receptors (MRs) modulate transcription via direct signal transduction pathways Once bound to hormone in the cytoplasm, MRs dissociate from chaperone proteins and translocate to the nucleus Activated MRs dimerize and bind to MR response elements on chromosomes, modulating expression of a select set of genes Mechanisms of cortisol action Glucocorticoid receptors (GRs) modulate transcription via direct signal transduction pathways Once bound to hormone in the cytoplasm, GRs dissociate from chaperone proteins and translocate to the nucleus Activated GRs dimerize and bind to GR response elements on chromosomes, broadly modulating expression of several hundred different genes (depending on cell type)

852eac0713417f314c4ac29c368c426aeb402840.text: https://xkcd.com/1173/ Learning objectives • Differentiate between C21 steroids and androgen and estrogen hormones • List the androgen and estrogen hormones and identify their relative affinity for their receptors • Identify the sources of androgen and estrogen hormones • Describe the biosynthesis pathways for androgens and estrogens • Know the mechanisms that facilitate circulation and inactivation of androgens and estrogens, and explain their effects on half-life • Understand the primary mechanisms of action of sex steroid hormones on their target cells Steroid nomenclature Androgens lose Steroid hormones all share the these carbons same basic 4-ringed, planar structure derived from their cholesterol precursor. Estrogens also lose this To track the enzymatic actions one during steroid synthesis and appreciate the structural differences between steroid hormones, it is somewhat useful to know their nomenclature: • A, B, C, and D rings • Carbon numbering Androgens are C19 steroids • Double bond location Estrogens are aromatic C18 steroids Androgen synthesis Androgen synthesis in general begins exactly as we saw in the adrenal glands, starting from formation of the obligate steroid precursor pregnenolone and progressing to androstenedione. DHEA-S and androstenedione in circulation often undergo peripheral conversion to more active androgens in target cells. The gonads in both men and women are major sites of androgen production. Gonadal androgen production The androgen producing cells of the testis (left) are the Leydig cells (aka interstitial cells), which are found between the seminiferous tubules (along with the capillaries). The androgen producing cells of the ovary (right) are the thecal cells, which derive from the stromal cells between the follicles and surround each developing follicle. https://digitalhistology.org Androgen production in the testis The primary hormone produced by the testis is testosterone, a potent androgen • Synthesized from locally produced androstenedione by type III 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase (17β-HSD3, ‘androgenic’ 17β-HSD) • but reduction reaction • Enters into circulation and binds the carrier protein sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG, ~60%) or albumin (~38%), with only 2% circulating free • Enters the seminiferous tubule and is bound by androgen binding protein • Also subject to peripheral conversion Peripheral conversion of testosterone Some target cells express the enzyme 5α-reductase, which converts testosterone into the even more potent dihydrotestosterone (DHT). • DHT is required for certain The A ring of testosterone is virilization processes, both in utero aromatized in certain target cells by and during puberty the enzyme aromatase to synthesize 17β-estradiol, a potent estrogen Aromatase converts androgens to estrogens Aromatase can convert multiple androgens, thus producing different estrogens. In the ovaries: • Androstenedione is converted to estrone by aromatase • Estrone is converted to 17β-estradiol by the enzyme by type I 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase (17β-HSD1, ‘estrogenic’ 17β-HSD, red oval in the figure). • higher affinity for estrogen reactant Endocrine cells and hormones of the ovary Thecal cells in the ovary produce androgens, primarily androstenedione. • Some of the androgen produced enters circulation, but most diffuses into ovarian granulosa cells and gets converted to estrogens The primary estrogen to enter circulation from the ovary is 17β-estradiol • In circulation, ~60% of estrogens are bound to sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG), ~20% to albumin, and ~20% circulate as free hormone During the luteal phase of the ovarian cycle progesterone secretion from luteinized cells rises significantly • Progesterone binds weakly to corticosteroid binding globulin and albumin Half-life, inactivation, and excretion • The half-life of androgens and estrogens is typically less than 20 minutes • Some evidence that testosterone and androstenedione may last longer, with half-lives up to an hour • The half-life of progesterone is ~5 minutes As with adrenal steroids, liver enzymes inactivate the sex steroids via glucuronide and sulfate conjugation, increasing solubility and reducing affinity for carriers, and thus increasing excretion is via the kidneys. • 17β-estradiol is also subject to conversion to less active estrogens Estrogen production over a woman’s lifespan Estrone and estriol are both weaker estrogen hormones than estradiol • Estrone becomes the dominant estrogen after menopause, since it can be produced by peripheral aromatase from adrenal-origin androstenedione • Estriol is produced by the placenta during pregnancy (even exceeding estradiol concentration) Mechanisms of sex steroid action The ‘canonical’ mechanism downstream of sex steroid receptor activation is the same as we saw for mineralocorticoids and glucocorticoids • Receptor dimerization, translocation to the nucleus, modulation of transcription There are two genes encoding nuclear receptors for estrogen, called ERα and ERβ There is a single gene encoding the progesterone receptor (PR) and a single gene encoding the androgen receptor (AR) • The androgen receptor is X-linked There are also multiple membrane-bound versions of estrogen and progesterone receptors (encoded by different genes) that activate other signaling pathways

1694289fa1a49e1c8f46f3b43e291a9b221adf9e.text: Endocrine Module – Introduction to Hormones At the end of the lecture students should be able to: • Distinguish between endocrine signaling and other forms of intercellular communication • Classify hormones based on their structure, solubility, & storability • Describe catecholamine biosynthesis • Describe the initial step of steroid biosynthesis • Explain the effects of carrier proteins on hormone activity and half-life • Classify hormone receptors based on their subcellular location and mechanism of action • Explain the factors that influence cellular sensitivity to hormones and cellular response to hormones
